coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence covalent interaction enhanced sensor for lead ion based on novel graphitic carbon nitride nanocones
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Hanqiang
Huang, Yihong
Zheng, Yulin
Zhou, Jiangcong
Wu, Quansheng
Zhang, Zhusen
Gan, Feng
Chen, Wuhua
coredata.dc:description: Novel graphitic carbon nitride nanocones (g-CNNCs) were synthesized for the first time in this study. The SEM, TEM, XPS and FT–IR were used to research the structure of the g-CNNCs. We found that the g-CNNCs showed high selective and sensitive for fluorescence enhancement detection of Pb2+ ion via covalent interaction. In addition, the g-CNNCs exhibit stable and specific concentration-dependent fluorescence intensity in the presence of Pb2+ ion in the range of 1–200 μmol·dm−3, and the limit of detection was estimated to be 0.0438 μmol·dm−3 (3S/k). More importantly, the g-CNNCs were used to detect practical samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphitic carbon nitride
Nanocones
Covalent interaction
Fluorescence enhancement
Sensor
Pb2+ ion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Pb2+ ion in environmental samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM and (B) TEM of g-CNNCs. Inset of (B) is the HR-TEM.
(A) XPS survey spectrum, (B) C1s spectra, (C) N1s spectra and (D) FT–IR spectrum of g-CNNCs.
(A) UV–vis spectrum and fluorescence spectra of g-CNNCs, (B) 3D contour map mode of emission wavelength vs. excitation wavelength.

(A) Fluorescence response and (B) fluorescence lifetime of g-CNNCs in the presence and absence of 100 μmol·dm−3 Pb2+ ion, (C) fluorescence selectivity of g-CNNCs in the presence of metal ions (50 μmol·dm−3).
(A) UV–vis spectra, (B) N1s spectra, (C) FT–IR spectra and (D) zeta potential of g-CNNCs in the presence and absence of Pb2+ ion.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of g-CNNCs in the presence of various concentrations of Pb2+ ion (0, 1, 5, 10, 15, 20, 50, 100, 150 and 200 μmol·dm−3), (B) linear relationship of F/F0 with the Pb2+ ion concentration (1–200 μmol·dm−3).
Detection of Pb2+ ion in tap water by spiked method (n = 3) and compared with AAS.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence covalent interaction enhanced sensor for lead ion based on novel graphitic carbon nitride nanocones

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Sun Yat-Sen University, Guangzhou 510275, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials, Longyan University, Longyan 364000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Zhangzhou College of Science & Technology, Zhangzhou 363202, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: H. Zhang, College of Chemistry and Materials, Longyan University, Longyan 364000, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel graphitic carbon nitride nanocones (g-CNNCs) were synthesized for the first time in this study. The SEM, TEM, XPS and FT–IR were used to research the structure of the g-CNNCs. We found that the g-CNNCs showed high selective and sensitive for fluorescence enhancement detection of Pb2+ ion via covalent interaction. In addition, the g-CNNCs exhibit stable and specific concentration-dependent fluorescence intensity in the presence of Pb2+ ion in the range of 1–200 μmol·dm−3, and the limit of detection was estimated to be 0.0438 μmol·dm−3 (3S/k). More importantly, the g-CNNCs were used to detect practical samples with satisfactory results.

Schematic illustration of graphitic carbon nitride nanocones fluorescence enhanced sensing Pb2+ ion.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, quantitative detection of hazardous metals ions in the environment is paid more and more attention because hazardous metals ions will affect the health of human body and the environment [1]. Among hazardous metals ions, lead (Pb2+) ion is one of the most common and toxic hazardous metals, and a small quantity of Pb2+ ion can result in serious diseases, such as nervous system dysfunction, anemia, reproductive dysfunction, and developmental disorders, etc. [2–4]. Thus, there is great necessary to develop a simple and rapid method with high sensitivity and selectivity to detect Pb2+ ion in real samples. At present, there are many methods for detecting Pb2+ ion, such as colorimetric method [5–7], electrochemistry method [8,9], atomic absorption spectrometry [10], fluorescence spectrometry [11–13], etc. And the fluorescence spectrometry received wide attention and research because of cheap instrument, simple operation, rapid detection, and high selectivity.

Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), metal-free semiconductors, has attracted widespread attention due to its excellent physical and chemical properties. Nevertheless, water-insolubility limits its further application in some fields, such as optical sensing, electrochemical sensing and fluorescence imaging. Therefore, a lot of researchers have devoted to the synthesis of g-C3N4 materials with higher water-soluble. In recent years, many forms of water-soluble g-C3N4 materials such as nanosheets [14–16], nanofibers [17,18], nanorods [19,20] and nanodots [21,22], were successful developed and applied in photocatalysis, electrocatalytic oxygen reduction, sensing, bioimaging, etc. As different forms of g-C3N4 materials have different properties, it is very important to synthesize different forms of g-C3N4 materials.

Due to the excellent biocompatibility, photostability, low toxicity and high quantum yields, water-soluble g-C3N4 materials were employed for the fluorescent sensor metal ions in most of the researches, such as Cu2+ ion, Fe3+ ion, Hg2+ ion, Ag+ ion and Cr6+ ion [23–28]. The fluorescent sensors majorly relied on fluorescence quenching interaction between g-C3N4 materials and metal ions.

Herein, we synthesized novel g-C3N4 nanocones (g-CNNCs) via acidification, ultrasonic and hydrothermal method for the first time. The surface of g-CNNCs contains a number of negatively charged oxygen–rich functional groups, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl. Furthermore, it can be observed that the Pb2+ ion could enhance g-CNNCs fluorescence intensity via terminal amino functions and CN and CN heterocycles of g-CNNCs with Pb2+ ion. In addition, it was further developed to high sensitivity and selectivity fluorescence quantitative detection of Pb2+ ion in real sample with satisfactory results.

Melamine, metal salts (Fe(NO3)3, CuSO2, HgCl2, Pb(NO3)2, BaCl2, CaCl2, CdCl2, CoCl2, MgCl2, Ni(NO3)2) were purchased from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). All reagents were analytical reagent grade and all the solutions were prepared with deionized water (18.2 MΩ).

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were observed by KYKY–EM8000F (KYKY TECHNOLOGY, China) and Tecnai G2 F20 S–TWIN Microscope (FEI Company, America), respectively. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT–IR) was analyzed on Thermo NICOLET iS 10 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). X–ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded by Thermo ESCALAB 250XI (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). Ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured on UV–2600 (SHIMADZU, Japan). Fluorescence spectra and fluorescence lifetime were carried out on RF–5301 (SHIMADZU, Japan) and FLS–980 (Edinburgh, UK), respectively. Zeta potential was measured by Zetasizer Nano (The Malvern Zetasizer, UK). Atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) was measured on AA–7000F (SHIMADZU, Japan).

Typically, 10 g of melamine was heated to 550 °C over 2 h, and maintained at 550 °C for another 2 h. The obtained yellow bulk compound (g-C3N4) was milled into powder. Next, 1.0 g g-C3N4 powder was sonicated in 20 mL H2SO4 for 4 h, then the homogeneous solution was diluted with 500 mL deionized and neutralized to neutral using NaOH solution. The obtained colloidal suspension was filtered to remove Na2SO4 and dried at 60 °C. Finally, 0.1 g as-obtained powder was re-dispersed in 20 mL distilled water under ultrasonication for 30 min and then transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-sealed autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling down to room temperature, this solution was sonicated for 6 h and filtered with a 0.22 μm microporous membrane, and a clear and colorless g-CNNCs solution was obtained.
100 μL g-CNNCs containing different concentrations Pb2+ ion (0–200 μmol·dm−3) was diluted to 10 mL, and the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded with excitation wavelength at 250 nm.
To evaluate the selectivity of Pb2+ ion, different coexisting metal ions (50 μmol·dm−3) were selected and recorded under the same condition.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM) were used to investigate the morphology of g-CNNCs. As shown in Fig. 1A and B, the g-CNNCs possess obvious cone structure with a size of several micrometers and random distribution. HR-TEM in the inset of Fig. 1B further illuminated that the morphology of g-CNNCs was cone structure.

X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) was recorded to research the chemical bonding of carbon atoms and nitrogen atoms of g-CNNCs. As shown in Fig. 2A, three dominant peaks at 284.6 eV, 399.6 eV and 531.6 eV were consistent with C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively, indicating the g-CNNCs contained carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements. The C1s spectra in Fig. 2B was spilt in two characteristic peaks at 284.79 eV and 288.75 eV, which were attributed to the C–C/C=C and N–C=N, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2C, the N1s spectra was decomposed into three peaks: 398.80 eV, 399.72 eV and 401.73 eV. The peak at 398.80 eV was assigned to sp2 hybridized aromatic N bonded to carbon atoms (C–N=C), the peak at 399.72 eV was attributed to tertiary N bonded to carbon atoms (NC3), and the peak at 401.73 eV was corresponded to terminal amino functions (C–N–H) [29–32].

Fourier transform infrared (FT–IR) spectrum was carried out to further examine the structure of g-CNNCs. It can be seen from Fig. 2D, the strong peak around at 3300 to 3500 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibrations modes of OH and NH. The characteristic peak around at 1655 cm−1 is corresponded to the typical bending vibration of CO and the characteristic peaks at 1400, 1438 and 1560 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations modes of CN and CN heterocycles. The broad peak located at 1115 cm−1 is corresponded to the stretching vibrations of CO, additionally, the peak at 807 cm−1 is assigned to the typical stretching modes of tri-s-triazine rings. This is consistent with the observation from XPS. The XPS and FT–IR research demonstrate that the amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl are present on the surface of g-CNNCs.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectra were carried out to investigate the optical properties of g-CNNCs. UV–vis absorption spectrum in Fig. 3A show a broad absorption band at 260–300 nm, which was belong to the π–π* electronic transition in the tri-s-triazine compounds [33,34]. Moreover, an emission peak can be observed at 365 nm when g-CNNCs were excited at 250 nm. In addition, the emission spectra of g-CNNCs with difference excitation wavelength were recorded and the 3D contour map mode was provided in Fig. 3B. This result revealed that the maximum emission wavelength located at 365 nm showed almost no change and which indicating that the emission wavelength of g-CNNCs is independent from the excitation wavelength.

We further examined the fluorescent responses of Pb2+ ion. It can be observed from Fig. 4A, the g-CNNCs shows an obvious fluorescent enhancement in the present of Pb2+ ion. In addition, the fluorescence lifetime of g-CNNCs with Pb2+ ion is stronger than the individual g-CNNCs (Fig. 4B), this result is in good consistent with the observed fluorescent enhancement. To evaluate the selectivity of g-CNNCs for Pb2+ ion, the influence of common metal ions including Cu2+ ion, Fe3+ ion, Hg2+ ion, Co2+ ion, Ni2+ ion, Cd2+ ion, Ca2+ ion, Mg2+ ion and Ba2+ ion were studied under the same conditions. The experimental results are provided in Fig. 4C, only Pb2+ ion cause significant fluorescence enhancement of g-CNNCs, and other metal ions cause negligible fluorescence change. It demonstrates that the excellent selectivity of g-CNNCs for the sensing of Pb2+ ion with satisfactory results can be obtained.

The UV–vis spectra, XPS, FT–IR and zeta potential were used to explore the reasons of high fluorescence selectivity toward Pb2+ ion. As shown in Fig. 5A, the absorbance of g-CNNCs gradually increases with the increase of concentration of Pb2+ ion, and a new absorbance peak can be observed at 258 cm, this result suggested that a new covalent compounds has formed by Pb2+ ion and g-CNNCs. Besides, as shown in Fig. 5B, the N1s spectra of g-CNNCs had shifted from 401.73 eV to 400.55 eV in presence of Pb2+ ion and the 401.73 eV was attributed to terminal amino functions, indicating Pb2+ ion bound to terminal amino functions of g-CNNCs. In addition, the FT–IR spectra of g-CNNCs were also recorded before and after the addition of Pb2+ ion, and the characteristic peak at 1400 cm−1 had transferred to 1384 cm−1 (Fig. 5C), which indicated that Pb2+ ion had bound to CN and CN heterocycles of g-CNNCs. What's more, zeta potential of g-CNNCs solution was about −22.1 mV, this may be caused by the negatively charged oxygen–rich functional groups (such as hydroxyl and carboxyl) on the surface of g-CNNCs. However, the zeta potential was turned into −27.9 mV when the Pb2+ ion was added (Fig. 5D). Combined with the N1s spectra and FT–IR, the cause of zeta potential transfer may be the reason of the Pb2+ ion has bound to heterocycles and terminal amino function of g-CNNCs simultaneously. All these results confirm that Pb2+ ion can bind to terminal amino and CN and CN heterocycles of g-CNNCs via covalent interaction.

To deeply investigate the sensitivity of g-CNNCs toward Pb2+ ion, a series of concentrations of Pb2+ ion were added in the g-CNNCs solution, and the fluorescence intensities were measured under the same condition. It can be seen from Fig. 6A, the fluorescence intensity shows continuous enhance with the addition of Pb2+ ion. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 6B, the F/F0 value showed good linearity with the concentration of Pb2+ ion in the range of 1–200 μmol·dm−3, and the linear regression equation is F/F0 = 0.0390c + 0.9447 (R2 = 0.9958). The limit of detection (LOD) at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 was estimated to be 0.0438 μmol·dm−3, which was lower than the national standard U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (0.075 μmol·dm−3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Pb2+ ion in environmental samples
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the feasibility of g-CNNCs for Pb2+ ion fluorescence sensors, the real samples (tap water) including Pb2+ ion were examined according to the standard addition method. The results are provided in Table 1 , and it can be found that the value of Pb2+ ion is basically the same with the added value of Pb2+ ion. In addition, the recovery is ranged from 98.73% to 102.60%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) is lower than 4.71%. Furthermore, the atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) was further used to verify the experiment results, and the result was consistent with the fluorescence experiment. These results demonstrate that the g-CNNCs could be efficiently applied for determination of Pb2+ ion with high accuracy.

In summary, we synthesized novel g-CNNCs through the combination of chemical and physical methods. The g-CNNCs show obvious conical structure and rich functional groups on the surface, such as hydroxyl, amino and carboxyl, and so on. In addition, the g-CNNCs displayed high selective and sensitive for fluorescence enhancing detection of Pb2+ ion. Most importantly, the g-CNNCs were used to detect practical samples with accurate results.
This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51702147).
The author(s) declare that they have no competing interests.
